subject: Accounting
chapter_name: Accounting for depreciation and disposal of non-current assets

note_data.title: Accounting for depreciation and disposal of non-current assets
keyPoints: **Depreciation** is the allocation of the cost of a non-current asset over its useful life
**Straight line method** calculates depreciation as (Cost - Residual value) ÷ Useful life
**Reducing balance method** applies a fixed percentage to the net book value each year
**Accumulated depreciation** is recorded as a credit balance in the ledger and shown as a deduction in the statement of financial position
When an asset is **disposed of**, the disposal account is debited with cost and credited with accumulated depreciation to find the net book value
**Profit on disposal** occurs when proceeds exceed net book value and is credited to the statement of profit or loss
**Loss on disposal** occurs when proceeds are less than net book value and is debited to the statement of profit or loss
The disposal account must be **balanced off** and any balance transferred to the statement of profit or loss
**Part exchange** involves trading an old asset plus cash for a new asset, requiring careful calculation of the allowance received

definitions.definition: The allocation of the cost of a non-current asset over its useful life to match expenses with revenues
The cost of an asset less accumulated depreciation
The estimated amount that could be obtained from disposal of an asset at the end of its useful life
The period over which an asset is expected to be used by the business
The total amount of depreciation charged on an asset from the date of purchase to the date of the statement of financial position

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all working clearly. Use the depreciation formula and show each step.
For disposal accounts, include all three elements: cost (Dr), accumulated depreciation (Cr), and proceeds (Cr)
Give the method + reason, e.g., 'Straight line method because it allocates equal amounts each year'

modelAnswer.question: A delivery van was purchased on 1 January 2020 for $24,000. It has a useful life of 4 years and a residual value of $4,000. The van was sold on 31 December 2022 for $8,500. Using the straight line method, calculate the profit or loss on disposal.
modelAnswer.answer:
Annual depreciation = ($24,000 - $4,000) ÷ 4 = $5,000
Accumulated depreciation (3 years) = $5,000 × 3 = $15,000
Net book value = $24,000 - $15,000 = $9,000
Proceeds = $8,500
Loss on disposal = $9,000 - $8,500 = $500

commonMistakes: Never subtract residual value when using reducing balance method - only subtract it in straight line method
Students often forget to calculate accumulated depreciation up to the date of disposal, not just for one year
Never call it 'depreciation account' - always use 'accumulated depreciation account'
Students confuse profit and loss on disposal - profit when proceeds > NBV, loss when proceeds < NBV
Never include depreciation for the year of disposal unless specifically stated
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every Paper 2 examination
Always show workings for depreciation calculations even if not asked
When preparing disposal accounts, use T-account format for clarity
Read disposal questions carefully - check if part exchange is involved
formulas: Straight line depreciation = (Cost - Residual value) ÷ Useful life
Reducing balance depreciation = Net book value × Depreciation rate %
Net book value = Cost - Accumulated depreciation
Profit/Loss on disposal = Proceeds - Net book value
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: T-account showing Asset Disposal Account with cost debited on left side, accumulated depreciation and proceeds credited on right side, with balancing figure being profit or loss on disposal
